file_name: Ennaji et al. - 2024 - Ensemble learning with weighted voting classifier for melanoma diagnosis.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an ensemble learning model with a weighted voting classifier for melanoma diagnosis in dermatology. Specifically, it employs three Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers trained on color, shape, and texture features extracted from dermoscopic images, with weights optimized using genetic algorithms. The model was evaluated on the PH2 dermoscopic database.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, introducing a novel ensemble-learning model with a weighted voting classifier for melanoma diagnosis. It details specific methodology (image decomposition, feature extraction, SVM classifiers, genetic algorithms), describes the dataset used (PH2 dermoscopic database), and reports original simulation results and performance metrics (99% accuracy, 97.5% sensitivity, 99% specificity). Although it includes a literature review section, its primary purpose is to report new technical findings and validation, classifying it as a research paper.

title: Ensemble learning with weighted voting classifier for melanoma diagnosis
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of melanoma diagnosis by proposing an ensemble learning model with a weighted voting classifier for dermoscopic image classification. The methodology involves decomposing images to extract color, shape, and texture features, which are then processed by three distinct Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers. Optimal weights for these classifiers are determined using genetic algorithms based on their performance metrics. The model was evaluated using the publicly available PH2 dermoscopic database, which contains 200 images of common moles, atypical moles, and melanomas. The proposed approach achieved an accuracy of 99%, a sensitivity of 97.5%, and a specificity of 99%. These results demonstrate that the weighted voting ensemble technique outperforms individual classifiers and majority voting methods, offering enhanced diagnostic accuracy for melanoma.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for diagnosis. The PH2 dataset contained 40 cases of melanoma. The model achieved 97.5% sensitivity for detecting melanoma cases.
A benign class included in the PH2 dataset. The dataset contained 80 common moles. These are grouped under 'non-melanoma' for binary classification metrics.
A benign/atypical class included in the PH2 dataset. The dataset contained 80 atypical moles. These are grouped under 'non-melanoma' for binary classification metrics.
A composite class representing non-cancerous lesions. It includes common moles and atypical moles from the PH2 dataset (totaling 160 images). The model achieved 99% specificity for this class.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between melanoma and non-melanoma (benign) lesions using dermoscopic images. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (cancerous or benign) using an ensemble of three SVM classifiers with weighted voting. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity on the PH2 dataset.

datasets_used.name: PH2 dermoscopic database
datasets_used.description: A publicly available database resulting from collaborative research between the Department of Dermatology at Pedro Hispano Hospital in Matosinhos, Portugal, and Porto University, Técnico Lisboa. It contains dermoscopic images acquired under standardized conditions with a consistent resolution of 768x560 pixels, corresponding to skin types II or III.

use.training: Used for training the three SVM classifiers (color, shape, texture) and optimizing weights via genetic algorithm. The dataset was split into a learning dataset comprising 80% of the images (160 images).
use.testing: Used for testing the model. The remaining 20% of the images (40 images) were used as the testing dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity on the PH2 dataset without statistical significance testing.

PH2 dermoscopic database.split: Learning dataset (80% / 160 images) and Testing dataset (20% / 40 images)
PH2 dermoscopic database.notes: The proposed approach with soft voting (weighted voting) achieved the highest performance among the six models examined. The model detected 39 out of 40 melanoma cases and 159 out of 160 non-melanoma cases. Results are based on a single evaluation on the PH2 dataset; no standard deviations or p-values were reported.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble learning model with weighted voting classifier

base_classifiers: Support Vector Machine (SVM) for color features
Support Vector Machine (SVM) for shape features
Support Vector Machine (SVM) for texture features
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Multiscale image decomposition (Osher-Vese model) separating object ('u') and texture ('v') components, followed by segmentation (Otsu’s thresholding) and extraction of color, shape, and texture features

classification_or_output_head.details: Combines outputs of three SVM classifiers based on weights optimized by genetic algorithms

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Ensemble learning model with weighted voting classifier
preprocessing_techniques: Partial Differential Equation (PDE) image decomposition using the Vese-Osher model to split images into object ('u') and texture ('v') components
Image segmentation using Otsu’s thresholding on the object component ('u') to detect the region-of-interest (ROI) and lesion masks
Strategic cropping to center the lesion for focused analysis
Multi-scale image decomposition for feature extraction
conclusions: The proposed ensemble learning model with a weighted voting classifier, utilizing three SVM classifiers trained on color, shape, and texture features with weights optimized by genetic algorithms, achieved 99% accuracy, 97.5% sensitivity, and 99% specificity on the PH2 dermoscopic database.
The weighted voting ensemble approach outperformed individual SVM classifiers (using color, shape, or texture features alone), feature fusion without weighting, and hard voting majority techniques.

The integration of multi-scale image decomposition with a weighted voting strategy enhances diagnostic accuracy and reliability for melanoma diagnosis compared to methods relying on single feature sets or unweighted voting.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding the reliance on the single PH2 database, which may introduce bias and limit generalizability, and the exclusion of deep learning methods in the current framework.
Future work will focus on integrating Vision Transformer (ViT) models to improve feature extraction and expanding the study to larger, more diverse datasets to ensure robustness.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the PH2 dermoscopic database, which limits generalizability as all images correspond to Fitzpatrick skin types II or III, potentially introducing population bias against darker skin types.
The dataset is small (200 images total) and imbalanced, with only 40 melanoma cases compared to 160 non-melanoma cases, which may inflate performance metrics.
No external validation was performed; results are based solely on a single internal split (80% training, 20% testing) of the PH2 dataset.
The paper does not report statistical significance testing, p-values, or confidence intervals for the reported performance metrics (99% accuracy, 97.5% sensitivity, 99% specificity).

The methodology excludes deep learning architectures (e.g., CNNs, Transformers) which are noted in the literature review as state-of-the-art, limiting the comparison to traditional machine learning approaches.
The paper acknowledges that future work is needed to integrate Vision Transformer (ViT) models and expand to larger, more diverse datasets to ensure robustness.
